summary: Cantrill announces Joyent's membership in the Cloud-native Computing Foundation, explaining his evolved thinking on open source foundations. While he previously viewed foundations as anti-patterns that eschew leadership, he now believes that when open source projects become foundational bedrock that multiple companies depend on, they genuinely need foundation governance to survive commercial pressures. He distinguishes the CNCF as a 'third model' - neither a single-project foundation nor an open source orphanage, but a mission-driven organization dedicated to advancing cloud-native computing values.
key_points: Engineering values of robustness and self-diagnosing systems become more important, not less, with cloud computing
When multiple companies bet their futures on an open source project, it needs foundation governance to survive
Commercial pressures on popular open source will 'rip it apart' without neutral foundation oversight
Single-project foundations aim too low; umbrella foundations risk becoming 'open source orphanages'
The CNCF represents a third model: mission-driven foundations with strong engineering values
money_quotes: The older I get, the more engineering values matter to me – and the more I seek out shared values in those with whom I endeavor to build things.

If multiple companies are betting their future on your open source project, congratulate yourself for laying down the bedrock upon which others are building – and then get it into a foundation to assure its future.
The commercial pressures that are now being exerted on your (incredibly popular!) technology will rip it apart if you don't preserve its fate.
Only a foundation can provide the necessary (but not sufficient!) neutrality to assure the community that the technology's future transcends the fate of any one company.

Foundations that are amalgamations of otherwise unrelated software seem to me to run the risk of becoming open source orphanages: providing shelter and a modicum of structure, perhaps, but lacking a sense of collective purpose.
Are we really to have a foundation for every GitHub repo that reaches a certain level of popularity?
key_insight: Open source foundations are necessary not as a default governance model, but specifically when a technology becomes foundational bedrock that multiple competing companies depend upon - and those foundations should be mission-driven rather than merely custodial.
